Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまなしおかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧郷社]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Yamanashioka Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №1.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまなしおかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧郷社]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまなしおかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧郷社]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧郷社]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Yamanashioka Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №1.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:山梨県の旧郷社]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Yamanashioka Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №1.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]]

In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:甲斐国の式内社]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]]

Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Yamanashioka Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №1.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}1 Shimo-Ishimori |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small), candidate shrine
Former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-plate roof |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashioka shrine Yamanashi-City in the Mt.Ishimori №2.JPG| thumb|200px|Haiden (photographed June 2010)]] '''Yamanashioka Shrine''' (やまなしおかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Yamanashi|Q840610|WD=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|lt=gōsha|1=Gō-sha}} that claims identification with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} "Yamanashioka Shrine"However, the common scholarly view identifies the Shikinaisha "Yamanashioka Shrine" with the shrine of the same name in {{ill|lt=Fuefuki|Q849434|WD=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}, a former gōsha.. The shrine stands on a small hill in flat land known as "Ishimori-yama" or "Ishimori-oka"There is a tradition that {{ill|lt=Daidarabotchi|Q1157529|WD=Q1157529|1=Daidarabotchi}} built it.. Though compact, the precinct has large trees such as pines and many strange rocks, and is known as a famous spot for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=azaleas|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was long loved by men of letters, and individual rocks were given distinctive namesIt is also pointed out that most of these rocks were once largely buried and only slightly exposed, so it is uncertain whether the site was called "Yamanashioka" at the time of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' compilation or earlier ("Survey Report").. === Shrine name === A folk etymology says the area, being lacking in relief, was called "yama-nashi" (no mountain), which later became "Yamanashi""Jinja Meisaichō," quoted in "Survey Report.". In addition, the '{{ill|lt=Kai Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}' of {{ill|lt=Bunka|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943|1=1814}}) records the then shrine name as "Kumano Gongen." === Enshrined deities === It enshrines six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q16283888|WD=Q16283888|lt=pillars|1=pillars}}: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotosakanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatamao-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}. The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' and '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Kokushi|1=UNKNOWN}}' of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|WD=Q7763|1=1787}}) say the shrine jointly enshrined {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} and '''Kunitachi Myōjin'''; the first three deities above correspond to the former, and the latter three to the latter. === Origin tradition === According to shrine tradition, the original founding year is unknown, but it is said {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} invited the deity during his expedition against the eastern peoples, and one tradition says it was founded by Yamato Takeru in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640|1=AD 111}})'Shintō Daijiten.'. East of the honden is the '''Mikoshikake-ishi''', said to be the stone where Yamato Takeru sat. It is further said that in {{ill|lt=Jingo-keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}), {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|Q3137722|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} built shrine buildings; later military figures also rebuilt them repeatedly, including {{ill|lt=Kagami Tōmitsu|Q11399726|WD=Q11399726|1=Kagami Tōmitsu}} in {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415|1=Bunji (era)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1187|Q19731|WD=Q19731|1=1187}}). In earlier times, a yearly archery rite was also held'Jinja Meikan.'. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held over 2 {{ill|lt=koku|Q855048|WD=Q855048|1=Koku}} 5 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} of shuinchi and over 3 koku 7 to of kokuinchi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked gōsha. === Subordinate shrines === * Tenjin-sha - enshrines Yamato Takeru; stands on top of the above Mikoshikake-ishi. 10 other shrines === Shrine architecture === The honden is a two-seat {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}} niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper-plate roofing. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=moya|1=UNKNOWN}} has gable-side ornamentation such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=gegyo|1=UNKNOWN}} and board-style {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kaerumata|1=UNKNOWN}}; the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tokyō|1=UNKNOWN}} are demi-bracket sets with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kobushi-bana|1=UNKNOWN}}; it has an engawa with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=giboshi|1=UNKNOWN}} railings, and rear side panels are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=waki-shōji|1=UNKNOWN}}. The one-bay {{ill|lt=kōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} uses chamfered square posts and is connected to the moya by an "ebi" {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|WD=Q124421222|1=kōryō}} beam. Decorative details preserve {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Momoyama-period|1=UNKNOWN}} techniques. It was designated a Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} on November 7, {{ill|lt=1960|Q3754|WD=Q3754|1=1960}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 35). In dismantling repairs the next year (1961), ink writing found behind a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daito|1=UNKNOWN}} indicated repair in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 16 ({{ill|lt=1703|Q6822|WD=Q6822|1=1703}}), making it a valuable dated structure'Yamanashi Prefectural History: Cultural Properties Volume,' Yamanashi Prefecture, 1999.. === Cultural property === * Honden (Yamanashi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), 'Japanese Gods: Shrines and Sacred Places,' vol.10 Tōkai (new reprint), {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02510-9 (first edition 1987) * Supervised by {{ill|lt=Miyaji Naoichi|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} and {{ill|lt=Saeki Ariyoshi|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, 'Shintō Daijiten (compact ed.),' {{ill|lt=Rinsen Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition by {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1940) * Jinja Honchō Research Dept. (ed.), 'Jinja Meikan,' 1963 {{commonscat|Yamanashioka-jinja, Yamanashi}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamanashioka Shrine, Yamanashi]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[category:山梨市の歴史]] [[Category:山梨市の建築物]]
